morning note. If you would like to

risk to the extent you haven't been
following kiss and Dr. Mo need to be a
client of 42 macro. So today's key macro
questions are how will pus reshape the
Fed and how should investors gauge the
impact of tariffs? Our answers,
President Trump signaled he will
nominate a temporary Federal Reserve
governor over the next two to three days
to fill the upcoming vacancy, but he
will save the announcement of Jerome
Pal's replacement as Fed chair for later
maintaining flexibility in reshaping the
central bank's leadership. Trump's
deliberations come amid three Federal
Reserve officials. San Francisco Fed
President Mary Dailyaly, Minneapolis Fed
President Neil Kashkari, and Fed
Governor Lisa Cook flagging softness in
the US labor market, keeping September
rate expectations alive. Where were
these people last week? We had Hey, hey,
Lisa and Neil and and Mary, we had an
FOMC last week in which you could have
voiced these opinions.
I digress.
These two late pivots come amid the Bank
of England cutting its policy rate by 25
basis points to 4%. the lowest in over
two years in a five-4 split that
required two rounds of MPC voting for
the first time in its 28-year history.
The rate cut came amid the NPC revising
it 2025 headline CPI forecast to 4% up
from 3.7%.
All told, the Bank of England represents
a model of a central bank that
understands the world is living through
a high sovereign indebtedness requires
high nominal growth regime. The world is
also undergoing a major major
geopolitical restructuring with respect
to trade and military readiness which
tends to occur during four turnings. And
this dynamic also adds to underlying
inflation pressure. Given these
preconditions, it would be foolish for
any central bank to stubbornly stick to
an arbitrary 2% inflation target and sty
growth throughout the entire process. We
can only hope the administration and the
Fed are paying attention to our thought
leadership on this subject matter. We
all know we have a very famous person in
the administration who used to be a
former client. So uh if you're listening
Scott uh please provide this at the flag
pole so we can boom this economy.
Elsewhere uh President Trump's
reciprocal tariff regime took effect at
midnight. He also signaled a 100% tariff
rate on semiconductors planned uh while
pledging exemptions for Apple and other
manufacturers who ship manufacturing to
the US after a visit to the Oval Office
by Apple CEO Tim Cook. You should know
by now how POTUS operates. Kiss the
ring, get the green. Our friends over at
the Yale Budget Lab estimate the average
effective tariff rate is now 18.3% which
is the highest since 1934. After
accounting for substitution effects, the
average tariff rate will be 17.3%, the
highest since 1935. The YO Budget Lab
further estimates Trump's tariffs will
bring in 2.7 trillion in revenue in the
10-year budget window ending 20 in 2035.
Their estimate declines to 2.2 trillion
after accounting for dynamic scoring.
Our friends in the Penn Warden budget
model currently project tariff revenue
over the same time horizon at 3% and two
and 2.6% trillion or sorry 3 trillion
and 2.6 6 trillion on a raw and dynamic
foreign basis respectively. These
figures imply the durability um I'm
sorry these figures imply durability to
the new tariff regime beyond the current
administration. It's going to be very
hard for the next president whether they
be Republican or Democrat to turn off
these revenues in the context of our you
know you know persistent structural six
to 7% you know budget deficits in terms
of budget deficit GDP ratio. So, uh, you
know, we're in a new world order here as
of midnight last night. And so, uh,
investors just need to to accept that
and move on. And I think the markets
will largely have in the context of
being forced to capitulate into our
paradigm C view, which is exactly what
we've been telling you it would do since
May 3rd. We set the market. So you're
going to see a wave of upside
capitulation as one by one fund A, fund
B, strategist A, strategist B, analyst
A, analyst C, B, C, D, EFG, they're all
going to be forced to realize that it's
not just about tariffs. It's about
trying to outgrow the deficits and the
debt. Now, we're not going to be
successful at this, but it could take
years before the plan fails, before
paradigm C fails. So ultimately uh you
know a lot of what we're seeing in asset
markets in terms of responding to the
